content.problemStatement:
**Scenario: Tracking New Updates Before Merging** \
You and your teammate are working on a project called **Blog-Website**. \
Your teammate recently made some updates directly on **GitHub** (remote repo):
- Added a new file: `about.html`
- Updated `index.html` with a new heading
- Pushed all changes to the `main` branch

**Your local repository does NOT have these updates, because you haven't synced** yet. \
But this time, instead of directly updating your working directory using `git pull`,
you want to **first check what changed before merging it** into your **local branch**.

---

**Your Task** (Using `git fetch`): \
**Step 1 — Fetch the latest updates from GitHub** \
Fetch the changes but **do NOT merge them automatically**. \
**Step 2 — Compare what changed** \
Check which commits were added in the remote main branch. \
**Step 3 — Merge the updates manually** \
After reviewing the fetched updates, merge the remote changes into your local main branch.

---

**Expected Output Before Merge :**
First check commit history using `git log`. \
Then Compare Your Local Git Main with GitHub Main. \
You'll see

These are the commits that your teammate has pushed to the Blog-Website GitHub repo, but you have not merged them into your local Git repo yet. \
**After merging, these commits will be synced with your local repository**. \
Merge and then check the commit history again, then observe what changed.

---


**Let’s see it visually :**
